flashcards.back: They contribute active cochlear gain, compression, and sharp frequency selectivity.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What does 20 log10 describe in acoustics?
flashcards.back: A sound-pressure amplitude ratio expressed in decibels.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What is the ideal distance rule?
flashcards.back: Doubling distance from a point source in a free field reduces level by about 6 dB.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Why use routine checks after annual calibration?
flashcards.back: They can reveal interim transducer, equipment, connection, or environmental problems.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What is a critical band?
flashcards.back: A psychoacoustic model of the frequency region over which masker energy strongly affects target detection.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What is upward spread of masking?
flashcards.back: Lower-frequency masker energy often masks higher-frequency targets more than higher-frequency energy masks lower targets.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What does temporal resolution support?
flashcards.back: Detection of rapid changes and brief gaps that contribute to speech perception.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Which binaural cue is prominent at low frequencies?
flashcards.back: Interaural time difference. Apply the concept using current ethical, legal, audiologic, educational, medical, technical, and jurisdictional requirements.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Can quiet thresholds predict all speech-in-noise performance?
flashcards.back: No; suprathreshold processing, environment, language, cognition, and other factors also matter.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: How should age inform audiology?
flashcards.back: Use developmentally and functionally appropriate methods while preserving individual variability and cross-checking results.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What comes before personal protective equipment in the control hierarchy?
flashcards.back: Reduce hazardous sound through feasible engineering and administrative controls.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Does a labeled attenuation rating guarantee field protection?
flashcards.back: No; actual attenuation depends on fit, use, environment, and verification.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Why can overprotection be a problem?
flashcards.back: It may unnecessarily reduce speech, warning-signal, and environmental access.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What supports valid threshold-shift review?
flashcards.back: Comparable testing plus calibration, reliability, middle-ear, exposure, and protocol evidence.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What is needed before ototoxic monitoring?
flashcards.back: A defined medical context, baseline when feasible, suitable measures, and coordinated follow-up.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What is practical recreational-risk counseling?
flashcards.back: Lower level or duration, increase distance, take breaks, use fitted protection, and evaluate persistent symptoms.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What does a hearing screen establish?
flashcards.back: Risk and need for follow-up, not diagnosis, eligibility, or a complete functional profile.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What follows a newborn refer result?
flashcards.back: Protocol-defined timely rescreening or diagnostic evaluation through the EHDI pathway.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Can a newborn pass end all surveillance?
flashcards.back: No; risk indicators or later concern can require monitoring for delayed-onset or progressive change.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Why control ambient noise in school screening?
flashcards.back: Noise can elevate apparent thresholds and increase invalid referrals.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What is loss to follow-up?
flashcards.back: Failure to complete the recommended next step after a refer or incomplete result.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Which metrics show pathway quality?
flashcards.back: Coverage, referral, diagnostic completion, timeliness, loss to follow-up, disparities, and data quality.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What does an air–bone gap suggest?
flashcards.back: A conductive component when measurements are valid and masking and calibration support the comparison.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Why is bone conduction not inherently ear specific?
flashcards.back: The oscillator can stimulate both cochleae, so masking may be necessary.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What does the plateau method seek?
flashcards.back: A stable test-ear threshold across increases in masking noise.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What should SRT usually cross-check?
flashcards.back: The pure-tone average, considering language, materials, and response validity.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What limits word-recognition interpretation?
flashcards.back: Presentation level, audibility, list properties, language, variability, and measurement error.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What does sound-field testing usually represent?
flashcards.back: The better responding auditory pathway, not an ear-specific threshold.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What helps interpret a flat tympanogram?
flashcards.back: Equivalent ear-canal volume, probe conditions, age, otoscopy, and clinical context.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Does an absent reflex always mean neural pathology?
flashcards.back: No; middle-ear transmission, degree of loss, technical factors, and the full reflex arc also matter.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What do OAEs primarily reflect?
flashcards.back: Outer hair-cell-related cochlear function under suitable middle-ear and recording conditions.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Do present OAEs prove normal hearing?
flashcards.back: No; they do not establish behavioral thresholds, neural synchrony, or speech understanding.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What does ABR measure?
flashcards.back: Time-locked neural activity evoked by controlled auditory stimuli.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Why cross-check electrophysiology?
flashcards.back: Each measure samples a different mechanism and carries technical and inferential limits.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Which history can require urgent escalation?
flashcards.back: New focal neurologic signs, severe acute symptoms, trauma, sudden hearing change, or other protocol-defined red flags.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What does caloric testing emphasize?
flashcards.back: Low-frequency horizontal-canal and related superior vestibular-nerve function.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What does vHIT emphasize?
flashcards.back: Higher-frequency vestibulo-ocular reflex function for the tested canals.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What do VEMPs sample?
flashcards.back: Otolith-related reflex pathways under specific stimulus and recording conditions.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Does tinnitus matching establish cause?
flashcards.back: No; it describes selected perceptual characteristics but does not identify one etiology.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What must APD differential reasoning consider?
flashcards.back: Peripheral hearing, language, attention, memory, learning, development, and environment.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What should happen when audiologic measures disagree?
flashcards.back: Check technical and task factors, identify plausible mechanisms, state uncertainty, and choose the next resolving measure.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Why may pediatric assessment require repeated visits?
flashcards.back: Reliable ear- and frequency-specific behavioral skills emerge over time and must cross-check physiologic estimates.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What is the response to suspected sudden sensorineural hearing loss?
flashcards.back: Urgent medical evaluation through current protocol.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What pattern can raise concern for auditory neuropathy spectrum?
flashcards.back: Preserved cochlear outer hair-cell evidence with abnormal neural synchrony, after technical confirmation.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Does a hearing diagnosis determine school eligibility by itself?
flashcards.back: No; educational impact, need, law, and team evidence guide individualized decisions.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What broadens classroom assessment?
flashcards.back: Noise, distance, reverberation, technology, language, participation, fatigue, partners, and student report.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What is verification?
flashcards.back: Objective confirmation that the device delivers intended electroacoustic output, often through real-ear measures.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What is validation?
flashcards.back: Evidence that the fitting improves the person’s real-world goals, function, and participation.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Why is manufacturer first fit insufficient?
flashcards.back: It does not account fully for individual ear acoustics or prove target match and safe output.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What does compression do?
flashcards.back: Maps a broad input-level range into the listener’s smaller usable output range.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: When can directionality help?
flashcards.back: When spatial arrangement lets the microphones favor the desired source relative to noise.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What should feedback troubleshooting include?
flashcards.back: Fit, coupling, wax, venting, receiver, leakage, programming, and verified feature behavior.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What does a cochlear implant stimulate?
flashcards.back: The auditory nerve through electrical stimulation delivered by an implanted electrode array.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Does implantation guarantee a specific speech outcome?
flashcards.back: No; outcomes vary with auditory, medical, developmental, language, rehabilitation, and personal factors.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What is a major remote-microphone benefit?
flashcards.back: Improved signal-to-noise ratio by reducing the effective distance to the desired talker.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Are remote microphones only for hearing aids?
flashcards.back: No; compatibility and routing vary across hearing aids, implants, receivers, and other access systems.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What is the first troubleshooting principle?
flashcards.back: Move systematically from power and physical connections to settings, accessories, source, and verified output.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: How is MRI safety determined?
flashcards.back: Use the exact device model and current manufacturer and medical conditions; never assume class-wide compatibility.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What makes rehabilitation person centered?
flashcards.back: Goals and supports reflect the person’s priorities, identities, environments, access needs, strengths, and outcomes.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: How does adjustment counseling differ from information giving?
flashcards.back: It addresses emotional response, identity, expectations, coping, and problem solving within scope.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Should language access wait for technology outcomes?
flashcards.back: No; timely accessible communication and language development remain priorities.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What helps classroom listening beyond seating?
flashcards.back: Acoustics, technology, visual access, instructional routines, peer access, and monitoring.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What is the audiologist’s school-team role?
flashcards.back: Contribute specialized audiologic and functional evidence within the authorized collaborative process.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What supports self-advocacy?
flashcards.back: Recognizing breakdowns, managing devices, requesting access, making choices, and practicing repair strategies.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What makes consent valid?
flashcards.back: Accessible material information, capacity and authority, questions, voluntariness, and continuing agreement under current law.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Does HIPAA govern every school audiology record?
flashcards.back: No; determine the setting, record holder, FERPA or HIPAA status, state law, and policy.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: Who retains accountability for delegated audiology services?
flashcards.back: The qualified supervising audiologist within applicable delegation and supervision rules.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What are the three EBP inputs?
flashcards.back: Best research, clinical expertise and data, and the individual’s values, culture, circumstances, and goals.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What supports ethical documentation and billing?
flashcards.back: Accurate, timely records and codes that truthfully represent the service and decision.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What must teleaudiology verify?
flashcards.back: Client-location authority, consent, privacy, accessibility, technical validity, local support, and emergency planning.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What is the recurring screening chain?
flashcards.back: Define the population and decision, use a valid accessible protocol, communicate pass or refer accurately, complete timely follow-up, and monitor loss to follow-up and disparities.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What is the recurring diagnostic chain?
flashcards.back: Clarify the referral question, collect technically valid cross-checks, integrate mechanism and function, state uncertainty, act on red flags, and connect conclusions to measurable follow-up.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

flashcards.front: What is the recurring safety boundary?
flashcards.back: Study results cannot diagnose, fit a device, determine vestibular or medical care, or replace emergency action. Use qualified individualized services, current protocol, informed consent, and timely referral.
flashcards.reviewNote: Source-reviewed independent study card; not official ETS or ASHA content.

memoryAids.title: Sound Path: Collect, Transfer, Transduce, Transmit
memoryAids.content: Outer ear collects, middle ear transfers, cochlea transduces, and cranial nerve VIII and central pathways transmit and process the signal.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: Cochlear Map: Base High, Apex Low
memoryAids.content: High-frequency traveling-wave maxima occur toward the basal cochlea; low-frequency maxima occur progressively toward the apex.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: Hair Cells: IHC Inform, OHC Optimize
memoryAids.content: Inner hair cells provide most afferent information; outer hair cells optimize sensitivity, compression, and frequency selectivity.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: Pressure Decibels: Twenty Log
memoryAids.content: For sound-pressure ratios, use 20 log10. Keep reference quantity and measurement conditions explicit.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: Binaural Cues: Low Time, High Level
memoryAids.content: Lower-frequency localization relies strongly on interaural time differences; higher-frequency localization relies strongly on interaural level differences.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: Screen, Then Complete the Path
memoryAids.content: A screen identifies risk; it does not diagnose. Every refer or incomplete result needs a clear, timely next step and tracking.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: Cross-Check Before Concluding
memoryAids.content: Compare behavioral, middle-ear, cochlear, neural, symptom, developmental, and functional evidence; investigate disagreement rather than averaging it away.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: Air, Bone, Then Mask When Needed
memoryAids.content: Air tests the available pathway, bone largely bypasses outer and middle ears, and masking controls possible cross-hearing.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: SRT Cross-Checks PTA
memoryAids.content: Speech-recognition threshold should generally agree with the pure-tone average when materials, language, responses, and measurement conditions are valid.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: Flat Tymp: Check Volume and Context
memoryAids.content: A flat tympanogram needs ear-canal volume, probe quality, otoscopy, age, history, and related findings before interpretation.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: OAE Is Cochlear Evidence, Not a Hearing Verdict
memoryAids.content: OAEs inform outer hair-cell function under suitable conditions; they do not establish behavioral thresholds, neural synchrony, or speech understanding.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: ABR Estimates, Behavior Confirms
memoryAids.content: ABR samples synchronized neural responses and can estimate sensitivity; stimulus and correction limits apply, and behavioral cross-check remains important.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: Vestibular Frequency Map
memoryAids.content: Calorics emphasize low-frequency horizontal-canal function, vHIT samples higher-frequency canal reflexes, and VEMPs sample otolith-related pathways.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: Red Flags Before Routine Testing
memoryAids.content: Sudden hearing change, acute neurologic signs, severe new vestibular symptoms, trauma, or protocol-defined danger signs require timely medical or emergency action.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: Verify the Fitting, Validate the Outcome
memoryAids.content: Verification measures electroacoustic delivery; validation measures benefit, participation, comfort, and goal attainment in real life.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: Compression Maps Inputs to the Usable Range
memoryAids.content: Wide dynamic range compression changes gain across input levels to improve access while respecting comfort and maximum-output limits.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: Remote Microphone: Shorten the Acoustic Distance
memoryAids.content: Putting a microphone near the desired talker can improve signal-to-noise ratio despite physical distance, but compatibility and use must be checked.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: Language Access Now, Choices Without Bias
memoryAids.content: Provide balanced information and timely accessible language while respecting culture, identity, family goals, and informed communication choices.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: Privacy Starts With the Setting
memoryAids.content: Identify the record holder, setting, applicable FERPA, HIPAA, state, and organizational rules, authority, and secure minimum-necessary disclosure.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.

memoryAids.title: EBP Is a Three-Way Integration
memoryAids.content: Combine critically appraised research, clinical expertise and data, and the individual’s values, culture, circumstances, preferences, and goals.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Retrieval cue only; verify the underlying evidence, current ethics, scope, law, medical or educational protocol, device instructions, and individual audiologic findings.
